commentBody: Recipe is poorly written.  Admittedly, I could have read through it a couple of times.  Unclear if garlic should simply be mashed or also chopped.  Pay attention to the 'chopped' vs 'minced' cilantro and red onions.  The chopped items go in the sofrito, the minced cilantro goes in the sour cream and the minced onion is used at the end with 1/2 of the chopped tomatoes to finish the dish.  Don't bother buying a medley of mushrooms - the taste of the mushrooms is lost in the sofrito.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Mushroom-Stuffed Baked Plantains Recipe
commentBody: The recommended mushrooms are NOT interchangeable: They're vastly different in texture & taste. And, it would be a huge waste of money to make this recipe with any of the suggested mushrooms, since there are many competing flavors in the recipe. Morels are expensive (and seasonal). Maitake,  although it can be cultivated, is also pricey. Shiitake is more expensive than baby bellas, which are nothing more than white button mushrooms grown in different conditions (same species-Agaricus bisporus).

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Ìlá (Okra Soup With Shrimp and Greens) Recipe
commentBody:
Folks, I have just made this dish precisely as instructed.  I am an experienced, 66 year old cook who has a broad pallet and have eaten all kinds of from many different styles and cuisines.  

I live in Houston, Texas, where we have a thriving food culture from all around the world.  I purchased the dried ground crawfish and the fermented locust beans from a food market called Wazobia African Market.  Both products were fresh and stored in refrigeration.

Just want to let you know that the fermented locust beans are quite pungent.  As is the ground crawfish, but not as much so.  As it turns out, the flavor of the dish when cooked retains the pungent odor, and the taste is not pleasant to either me or my partner. (We both love okra in many forms!)  We have chosen not to eat it.  I am sure it is well loved by many, and that is great.  I simply want to say if the aroma of the special ingredients is not pleasing to you, I expect the flavors will not be as well.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Ìlá (Okra Soup With Shrimp and Greens) Recipe
commentBody: I made this dish yesterday with the ingredients I could find. I’m based in a small town in the Netherlands, so I couldn’t find fermented locust bean or crayfish powder. However, a previous commenter suggested subbing locust bean with red miso. I used the white miso I had at hand and found shrimp paste in an international market. I added goat meat and chicken thighs to mine, and it came out wonderfully. I only wish I added more okra, but that’s a personal preference. I will definitely make this again and hope to see similar recipes like this in the future.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Fish and Lentils With Sauce Rof Recipe
commentBody: In general—delicious. And. It’s summer!! It was a hot evening here. Guests outside. Me inside over a hot stove? No thank you. So. I simmered the soaked lentils with water in the same way I do for lentil salads I make. I skipped the extra oil. (Went outside and enjoyed a glass of wine with my friends.) When they (the lentils) still had a nice bite to them (resistance), I drained them and put them in a wide serving platter and added the rof. Then I put the marinated cod drizzled with EVOO in the oven (in a deep pan uncovered) 375? That’s what I chose—Not sure that’s the best option. But the cod was juicy.  About 18 minutes for 3.5 lbs.  check it to make sure it breaks apart with a fork. Added spinach at last minute and covered. Watch those pan handles coming out of the oven!!! Served with zucchini and shallots, roasted peppers, naan, tzatziki…I love the “confetti” of rof. It’s celebratory.

commentBody:
My spouse had a heart attack last month so we have been searching for intensely-flavored heart-healthy food that makes him happy. This rof sauce is all that. 

I make the lentils separately, the rice in the rice cooker and the rof sauce all on its own. Those three ingredients live in the refrigerator until we are ready to throw them together with fish, chicken, tofu or all by themselves. Or, I mix some of the rof sauce with Greek yogurt for variety. I use Chico blonde rice.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Fish and Lentils With Sauce Rof Recipe
commentBody: This is one of the most delicious dishes I’ve ever made! I would serve this to my most discerning guests. That sauce—think I would call it a salsa, though—is everything. I used Serrano peppers and seeded one of them, and the heat was on the just detectable. For my fish, I used tilapia, and instead of spinach, I used Swiss chard, because that’s what I had home. While the chard was good, spinach or Lacinato kale would be better. My only small problem was with the lentils: some of them got a little crispy on the bottom as the fish was finishing cooking. I think I’d use 3 cups of water next time or just pay attention and add water when they look dry. I also didn’t add as much salt. I didn’t salt the fish and I didn’t add the salt to the greens before they wilted, and I can’t imagine needing the extra salt from those 2 steps. I served roasted potatoes alongside, which was perfect, if perhaps unconventional.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Fish and Lentils With Sauce Rof Recipe
commentBody:
This idea is good, but the recipe and instructions are not good. I used black lentils and this came out FAR too soupy. My fish (16 oz of cod) took several minutes longer to cook than listed.

Other issues:
* the salting is imprecise and relies on too much salt in the sauce rof vs properly salting the other components
* marinating and cooking the sauce rof loses most of its flavor -- it's much better as a topping
* there's really no reason to add 1/4 C oil to the lentils or to soak the lentils

commentBody: I wanted to make half the recipe - I was fine until I got to the lemon. Can someone clarify it?

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Fish and Lentils With Sauce Rof Recipe
commentBody:
This was a delicious recipe. The first time I cooked it, I had some salmon that I grilled gently instead of cooking it with the lentils. Still put the sauce on, but added spinach to the lentils. It was really good.

The second time, I used cod and followed directions . Also delicious. I used a shallot instead of two of the 6 garlic cloves. I found both times the lentils were a tiny bit too salty. So I didn’t salt the fish after marinating it. I will reduce the salt for next time.

"Jeera" is the Hindustani name for cumin. In the 1800s, the British brought Indian and Chinese indentured laborers to the Caribbean; indenture was one level above slavery, which had been abolished in Britain in 1834. Caribbean dishes such as roti or dal puri are of Indian origin; Chinese immigrants contributed soy sauce and stir-frying.
For just 1 potato, microwaving the cubes for 6-8 mins is simpler.
Zesting the orange rather than adding/removing the peel avoids the bitter pith.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Beef Patties Recipe
commentBody:
The recipe sounds amazing, I can't wait to try it!  I love Patties of all types and origins!  It will be easier with the puff pastry. Great job of putting together your take on the restaurant's delicious dish. 
Just a clarification for the first comment- Cumin or Jeera is an annual herb grown to harvest the cumin seed, which is commonly used as a spice in Asian, Mexican, Indian, and Middle Eastern recipes. Cumin is native to the Mediterranean. Seeds found in Syria have been dated back to the second millennium BC and there are many references to cumin in Biblical text of the Old and New Testaments. It was introduced to the Americas by Spanish and Portuguese colonist. Today the plant is commercially grown in the Indian subcontinent, Northern Africa, Mexico, and Chile. So it has been around for awhile, and used by many cultures.
The potato cooked as described in the recipe is to use the potato water to add moisture and the natural starches to thicken the mixture. Boiling produces a better starchy water than microwaving.

I'll be cooking this, this weekend, and adding some peas and carrots!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Beef Patties Recipe
commentBody: This was a fun and delicious project.  I didn't see any other comments from people who live in places where we are thrilled to be able to get Pepperidge Farm puff pastry so don't complain about not being able to get Dafour, so on our behalf I will say, PF worked great! I used one box/two sheets, and cut each into 10 pieces, which worked out perfectly for the amount of filling.  Used a habanero (with seeds) instead of Scotch bonnet, and it seemed like a good amount of spice, if you're ok with spice. Takes some time, but all good!  And pretty!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Beef Patties Recipe
commentBody: Grew up on these. Every Caribbean island has a version of this. In Trinidad, they’re called beef pies. This filling sounds wonderful, but for less fuss, no potato (potato is Jamaican beef patty), season with salt, garlic, onion, any hot pepper, parsley, thyme, oregano, or an Italian spice mix. Cilantro, even. That simple. And don’t overstuff the filling. The joy is the buttery flakiness of the pastry against the savory earthiness of spicy beef. You can use any ground meat.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Eru (Cameroonian Stewed Greens) Recipe
commentBody: Thank you so much for bringing back memories of this delicious, nutritious dish. I was a Peace Corps volunteer in Cameroon in the late 1970s. I ate this dish at least weekly for two years and it provided me vegetables and protein in an otherwise limited and monotonous diet. While I’m sure there are many variations, the ingredients in this recipe matches quite closely to how it was prepared for me nearly 50 years ago.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Eru (Cameroonian Stewed Greens) Recipe
commentBody:
Highly adaptable. The essentials are red palm oil and some kind of oily smoked fish. I used a variety of greens -- kale, radish greens, pea shoots, and spinach -- all from the local CSA.  I happened to have smoked alewives on hand, but mackerel or sardines would likely work here as well.

Similar to many dishes of stewed greens and smoked or dried fish found across West and Central Africa. As with the previous commenter, it brought back many fond memories of time spent in that part of the world.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Poulet DG (Cameroonian Chicken and Plantains) Recipe
commentBody: I just happened to have all these ingredients in my refrigerator when this recipe came out. It was delicious! My only suggestion is with how to handle the plantains. My plantains were very ripe so I sautéed them in about two tablespoons of oil which browned them perfectly. I put them in with the vegetables as directed and felt like they broke down a little too much. Next time I will wait to add them with about 5 minutes left to cook on the dish. My wife doesn’t eat chicken so I took out some sauce and veggies and added it to some baked tofu for her which she enjoyed even though it wasn’t purely vegetarian.

commentBody: Lots of ingredients. Lots of prep time. Just good, not amazing. Didn't use Better than Bouillon and that was probably why it was just good. I added with the liquid with the chicken towards the end. I probably would have rated it lower but I like that it is a flavor profile I've never tried before.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Poulet DG (Cameroonian Chicken and Plantains) Recipe
commentBody: I've got a lot of problems with this recipe, and now you're gonna hear about it. Way too many steps involved. The marinade is too thick to really envelope and soak in. Skip the marinating completely, just start cooking and use skinless, boneless thigh meat, already cut up. The plantains get lost in there. I'd recommend using eggplant instead in the dish and serve plantains on the side. And if you like spicy, add at least 4 hot peppers. Following the recipe was tasty, but not worth the effort.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Poulet DG (Cameroonian Chicken and Plantains) Recipe
commentBody:
Lots of steps but SO worth it. This is probably one of the best things I’ve ever cooked. Made it for dinner on a Monday but will absolutely serve it to guests one of these days. The layers of flavor are incredible. And I don’t even remotely like bell peppers. 

Made a few adjustments as I went along: 
- Seared the chicken before cooking the marinade
- Added a jalapeño and a serrano to the chile and carrot because we like SPICE in this house 
- Added an extra plantain 
- Used a ton of rosemary, oregano and sage for my bouquet garni because that’s what I had in my yard

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody:
Ended up here from the very moving story, am a huge fan of the radio bakery corn cake, happened to have all the ingredients already so gave a whirl. 
I do think turbinado sugar or potentially something similar helps w the structure of the meringue. It is much fluffier and sturdier than meringues I’ve made with white sugar which I suspect helps it hold up to all the things that get mixed in. 
I used frozen sour cherries and bobs red mill medium coarse corn meal. I rested in the fridge for ~24 hours. 
The cake did not turn out great for me though. I ended up keeping it in the oven 10-15 minutes longer but it was still rather underbaked and the visual cues she describes in the recipe (including my cake tester) were reached. I am an avid baker and very familiar w the ways my oven misbehaves, so I don’t think my oven temp is wildly off. I also found the cornmeal to be far too gritty in the end for my taste. Maybe this is because it wasn’t fully baked? Or perhaps my cornmeal was too coarse- I suspect medium varies quite a lot.
It wasn’t fully baked delicious but texture all over the place (part too soft raw cake, part too crunchy raw corn meal)

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody: Having read the recipe and all the comments, I am wondering if the reason the cakes have come out rather coarse from the cornmeal is that the bakers are putting their cakes in the oven too soon.  As I read Yolanda‘s recipe and directions she suggests that the baker refrigerate the batter for up to THREE days. It seems to me that three days would help moisturize that cornmeal, especially if you are using, as she suggests a coarse cornmeal. I am 80 and don’t bake much anymore but I do love reading recipes. My granddaughter comes over and is the 16 year-old Baker in our house right now. She loves cooking and baking with her granddad and myself. I am the overseer much like Yewande and her granddad is the helper and sous chef. We have a grand messy time!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody: What a gem of a recipe! Really surprised that so few ingredients could produce such a rustic yet complex cake with a symphony of texture and flavor. The interior was pillowy soft and the exterior had the crackly crunch of toffee from the caramelization of the turbinado sugar. I agree with @vivien, this cake stands on its own and really doesn’t need the addition of the cherries. Next time I will bake this without the fruit, but will prepare a bowl of macerated berries to pass around the table. I followed the recipe to the letter, using a 30 hour resting/hydration time. I served with unsweetened whipped cream because the turbinado in this recipe provides plenty of sweetness. Bravo! Cake Komolafe is a new family favorite.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I halved the recipe and baked it in a shallow 10 inch cast iron skillet for 35 minutes. The texture is great as others described. But I find this cake too sweet. I wish I had not coated the pan with more sugar, at least. 
I used relatively fine corn meal and let the batter rest for 14 hours. This was sufficient. 
Some readers wondered why the egg whites are first whipped and then butter added, which might deflate the batter. I found this not to be the case. Trust the recipe. 
I wonder if one can reduce the amount of sugar without compromising the consistency of the batter.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody: I am a fan of the radio bakery corn cake. I can see the resemblance but I don’t think this recipe cracks the code for the home baker.  I read through the comments and made the folllowing adjustments- used cornmeal to coat the sides of the skillet and added a layer of parchment paper in the bottom baked slightly higher at 380 for an additional 15 mins. Cherries did not sink. It came out looking like a clafoutis & dislodged beautifully. However it’s still mushy and underbaked in the middle =unsightly from the flip. The edges are deeply burnished and crusty. My take on the problem is the skillet. I frequently bake a hot water cornbread with a coarse ground meal it is a very wet batter and one of the essentials is pouring the batter into a pre-heated skillet. I think the cast iron is not able to reach a high enough temp at 375 for 30 mins to thoroughly bake this dense batter. If I were to do it again- I’d use a thinner dark metal cake plate with the same prep which would allow better heat circulation. I put mine back in the oven at 200 for 45 mins to dry out. Should work but now it looks unfortunate from the handling and not nice enough to serve. Not sure if the cherries help or distract.

The only variation was using blueberries instead of cherries as I dislike cherries.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody: I wanted this to taste so good, and it did, but I wound up with a cake that was uncooked on the inside and too crunchy, like hard candy on the outside. I used Bob's Red Mill Medium Coarse corn meal but it never softened enough and the grains didn't absorb the liquid. As I said, the taste was good -- the brown butter flavor shone through in the uncooked "mush" but other parts were inedible. I want to try again, maybe with corn flour and less or even no sugar in the pan before baking.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody:
While my batter was resting, I read every single comment on this recipe and became anxious my cake would turn out badly. It was absolutely delicious: tender on the inside with a chewy, caramel-like exterior.

Taking into account the comments that said the cake was too sweet, at Step 8, I coated the sides of the pan with cornmeal instead of sugar and omitted the final sprinkle of sugar on top. I also think the grittiness mentioned by a few people is a deliberate feature, not a flaw.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody: Preparation takes a bit of work (longer than the recipe suggests) but the outcome is an interestingly textured and tasty cake. I used carton liquid egg whites and cane sugar to skip the food processor step. Bob’s red mill medium ground cornmeal. Rested for 32 hours. Baked for 40 min. Result was a deep golden brown top with a heterogenous texture inside, some chewy (reminiscent of meringue) parts and some cornbread texture parts. The cornmeal was clearly detected when chewing but we liked it. It was sweeter than expected so I agree with the comment to use cornmeal to coat the pan instead of more sugar. There was also absolutely no way that it would have popped out of the pan after baking. Super stuck on so served directly from the cast iron. With ice cream! Would make again.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to love this, but it’s just…okay. The cake is way too sweet, and despite (accidentally) baking it for slightly longer than I was supposed to, it’s underbaked; almost like a corn pudding rather than a corn cake. I let it rest for three days so the texture of the corn was nice. And the sugar around the crust was a nice addition for taste and crunch. But my goodness it’s borderline disgustingly sweet. I still love Yewande though and this is the first of her recipes that didn’t deliver for me.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Brown Butter Cornmeal Cake Recipe
commentBody: I was thrilled when I realized I already had turbinado sugar (I get mine at Trader Joe’s) and cornmeal so I made this today. It’s hanging out on the cooling rack and my partner and I can’t stop taking bites as we walk through the kitchen. I refrigerated it for 3.5 hours (I know, but we really needed a pick me up today) and omitted the cherries. I used a 10in cast iron skillet and ended up baking it for 45 minutes. The edges are incredibly chewy and crispy and I love the texture the sugar adds. The center is soft and gooey in the best way. I doubt this will make it through the weekend! Thank you for sharing your story and this recipe, I look forward to trying the Radio Bakery corn cake the next time we’re in NYC.

recipeTitle: Bobotie (Meat and Egg Casserole) Recipe
commentBody:
A thick egg topping resembling a frittata isn’t traditional- way too many eggs. Half that amount would suffice. 
Mrs Balls chutney is imperative- both inside the Bobotie mixture and for serving as a sambal accompaniment. Coconut & banana & chopped tomato/red onion mix is our family favorite. 
I don’t use Garam Marsala or chili - I 
use a mild curry powder ( Smart & Final have a good mild curry powder perfect for Cape Malay dishes - not spicy but imparting delicious flavor)

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Bobotie (Meat and Egg Casserole) Recipe
commentBody:
Bobotie is supposed to be a dish that uses whatever leftover meat you have. I really dislike ground beef, so we usually use ground pork, but turkey would also be fine. I’ve even made it with bison - it’s always delicious, and my American husband’s favorite thing to eat.

The egg custard here is way too thick. Part of the issue is the use of heavy cream. Way too fancy for this humble dish, and completely unnecessary. The egg custard should be thin and light, with pieces of browned meat poking through.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Bobotie (Meat and Egg Casserole) Recipe
commentBody: Considered an iconic South African recipe, Bobotie almost always includes a slice or two of crustless white bread soaked in milk as a binder to keep the minced meat mixture moist and tender. Squeeze the milk out of the bread and use the leftover milk (topped up) for the egg topping (no cream) and 3 eggs maximum. Spicing the meat varies by recipe but should always include curry powder and a little lemon juice, chutney, apricot jam, dried fruit and perhaps slivered almonds. Best to look for an authentic South African recipe to experience its amazing flavors! Done right, it’s delicious and enjoyed by non-south Africans too.

recipeTitle: Bobotie (Meat and Egg Casserole) Recipe
commentBody: Have had Bobotie all my life starting in Zimbabwe, now cooked by me in California.  Laurens  van der Post's African Cooking (NY: Time Life Books) has an excellent recipe p 40 (book p 139) with lean ground lamb, fresh lemon juice, chopped tart apple (like Granny Smith) seedless raisins, and blanched almonds; don't forget tucking in fresh orange leaves (or bay leaves).  Book version adds half hard boiled eggs within meat.  Newer recipe with ground beef is in The African Times Cookbook (Penguin).

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Red Red (Pepper Stewed Beans) Recipe
commentBody: Made this for a vegan guest, so obviously- no dried fish.  But as I looked at it, there’s so much of that, it’s not going to be truly optional.  So it wasn’t.  Vegan part of the stew showed potential, but it was missing:  bulk, umami, and other flavors.  So I rigged up a sautee of:  chopped celery, 3 cloves garlic (last), and soaked, chopped tofu skins.  Taking a deep breath, I added soy sauce the skins soaked up.  Fusion- and it was good.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Roasted Kelewele (Spiced Plantains) With Crispy Shallots and Herbs Recipe
commentBody:
I am wondering about the quantity of orange juice.  With no prior context for making this type of recipe, I followed things pretty closely, but instinctually the 1-1/4 OJ+zest (which was just 2 oranges, not 4) seemed too much liquid.  Ultimately that seemed correct and I ended up decanting most of it at the end so that the final broil could really caramelize and crisp the plantains.

Overall this was still delicious and will make again, but will reduce the juice at least 50%, and maybe bake uncovered a bit longer?

Wondering of others’ experience/thought on this matter, or if it really IS supposed to have so much juice.

commentBody: This is delicious.  I made a half size recipe and cut back a bit more on the orange juice (per the other comments I read) to about 1/2 cup vs 5/8 cup. Paired it with black beans as suggested and a large fresh salad from our home lettuce tower and our microgreens growing tray.  Hubby and I agree it’s a winner!  Thanks so much.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Pasta in Rundown Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Not really Caribbean unless it’s a mixup of various islands plus SE Asia.  To the best of my knowledge, we don’t use lemongrass, at least not in Jamaica, not even sure if most of us from the Caribbean know what anchovies are.  
My mom’s recipe is tomatoes, coconut milk, onions, hot pepper, garlic, thyme if wanted.  Cook until sauce separates, it will look curdled.  Add seafood at the end. We can also add tropical root vegetables instead of the pasta.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Pasta in Rundown Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I found this surprisingly tasty.  I did cook the pasta separately (8 oz for 2 people) as I was worried about overcooking it . I also added a head of broccoli cut into florets towards the end and took both off the heat when al dente. I used 1/3 of the anchovies and cooked the tomatoes a bit before adding half the coconut milk and reducing until thick and adding the pasta.  I did not add cheese as that seemed strange. The broccoli made it and the addition of shrimp would be great as well!

commentBody: Those who are complaining about the pasta cooking time without having made this (or similar recipes where the pasta cooks in the sauce): Use the recommended pasta shapes, which are sturdier, and follow the cooking times/temperatures, and the pasta and sauce come out with a great consistency. I cannot speak to the authenticity of the recipe, but the cooking technique works fine. The only thing I will say for recipes where the pasta cooks in the sauce, such as this one, is that the pasta tends to start sticking to the bottom of the pot towards the end, so stir and keep an eye on that.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Pasta in Rundown Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
I love Yewande.  She understands coconut milk in a spiritual way.  Reading her recipes makes me want to hang out with her. This dish satisfies a desire for tomato sauce that cheers up this insane cold weather in a fresh but also warming way.
Naysayers- you just like complaining.
I never have everything exactly on hand and she still leads me to  favorites that stay on rotation. 
This is now on the playlist.
 Jarred minced ginger and some fish sauce (to my taste-no anchovies) were my only substitutions. So yummy and easy. 

Even the picky eaters were happy.  For protein we paired with a jar of jalapeño oil tonnino and served a yogurty citrus salad on the side.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Isidudu (Creamy Corn Porridge) Recipe
commentBody: Adding the cornmeal this way, it's tricky to avoid lumps. But if you bring most of the water to a boil, combine the cornmeal with the rest of the water cold, and stir that in quickly, it won't lump (stir till it comes back to boil and starts to thicken). Hold back 1-1/2 c cold water and mix with the 1-1/2 c cornmeal just as the other water boils (i.e., not too far ahead of time).

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Savory Holiday Nut Mix Recipe
commentBody: I tried this before realizing that there was no instruction for the orange juice. So I made it without, and it lacked flavor. I tossed the finished nut mix in the orange juice and returned it to the oven, same settings as before, followed by a 20 minutes convect at 200F (dehydration). Better, but still not great. recipeTitle: Savory Holiday Nut Mix Recipe
commentBody: I too was disappointed by this recipe. I took the suggestions in the comments about adding a bit of sugar, adding a drizzle of EVOO, and also doubled the spices. A lot of time/effort for not a lot of payoff. I would try something else next time.

recipeTitle: Savory Holiday Nut Mix Recipe
commentBody: I've made this three times now, once using a zester for the orange rind and twice following the instructions to use a knife and avoid the white pith in the rind. author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Gúgúrú àti Epà (Sweet-Salty Popcorn and Peanuts) Recipe
commentBody: Just made this, and it was so good! It’s been years since I made popcorn on the stovetop. My first attempt resulted in unpopped kernels in burned sugar. I think I dropped the heat too low after adding the kernels and spices, and then burned everything trying to get back to the right temp. For the next two batches, I used a pan with a glass lid so I could see what was going on. Did not turn down the heat as much after adding the kernels and spices, and they starting popping within a minute. After that, vigorous shaking side to side and up and down. No unpopped kernels and nothing burned. I increased the sugar, salt and smoked paprika a bit on my third batch. I’ll be making this again soon!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Gúgúrú àti Epà (Sweet-Salty Popcorn and Peanuts) Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight. We have a Whirley-Pop stovetop popcorn pot, so I adapted timings for that. It was tasty! My paprika is a blend of 2:1 spicy smoked and sweet smoked Spanish paprika (from a bulk food store). Because there’s so little of it (1/4 tsp) I don’t know that the variety makes a major difference - use what you prefer. It also isn’t overly sweet (not like commercial kettle corn). One note: step 3 says to “break up any clusters” but there were no clusters at all. I’d be interested to know what other whirley-pop users experience. Next time I make this I might mix the popcorn with the sugar-spice-salt mix before putting it in the pot, to see if that makes a difference. (Also, I only had 1/2 cup peanuts in the pantry so added 1/2 cup toasted pecans - yum!)

commentBody: This one is a winner. The interesting combination of ingredients and flavors caught my attention. It definitely delivered. I did not have a habanero so I substituted sambal oelek, and I used dried basil during the simmer instead of fresh basil at the end. author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
I cooked this and liked it, but will save the stars until I do it correctly!

Even wrong it was good, but with Holiday Brain it took a couple minutes of "these are the largest anchovies I've ever seen..." to realize I'd just put in 8-10 *sardines*. 

I can imagine when I use the correct amount of the correct tinned fish it will be a lot more balanced! But if you find yourself in a pinch and don't mind bonus umami or like fish, just know it's still good if you goof up and do a ton of sardines.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Feedback: used large red onion because, why not?  Jalapeno, seeded/deveined: nice bite, not too spicy.  8 anchovies = too many, dominated flavour; 4-5 next time or skip.  1 tbsp miso was plenty.  Did 9 minutes smooth side, 3 minutes after flip. 4-5 minutes more after adding chicken back in.  Added water as sauce was getting a little dry.  Flavour was very good once you got past the slight dominance of the anchovies. No mushrooms; as a small child my father was killed by a large mushroom.

recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Four stars.  My intent was to make this as written.  When I opened the jar of roasted red peppers in my ‘frig, it looked like a biology experiment, so I used a fresh red peppers.  I had no scotch bonnet peppers, so I used 1/8t of cayenne for a half batch.  IMO, that was sufficient.  The dish was very good.  I should have put the diced fresh red peppers in with the onion/garlic rather than with the mushrooms.  Something to remember.  I will make this again with roasted red peppers.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
Made this in an Instant pot (10 min @ high pressure) with the following changes to adapt to what I had on hand:
Yellow onion instead of red
Jalapeno instead of habanero
No anchovies
Peanut butter instead of miso
Sauteed two red bell peppers instead of the jar of roasted peppers
1/2 cup water
Added a bunch of chopped kale to give it some color
No basil or cilantro (didn't have any)
Juice of 1/2 a lime

It came out great!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
Anchovies and miso, huh? I betcha the original West African recipe has dawadawa/soumbala, an umami packed made from fermented seeds that stinks to high heaven and tastes like Heaven. It can be found in African shops in big cities. 
Or you can make it yourself like I did - a long, extremely labor intensive process but a fun worthwhile project. I used seeds from palo verde trees that are abundant where I live in Tucson Arizona.

recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
We made this for dinner two nights ago, with two minor changes. We followed the directions up the point of returning the chicken to the pan. At that point, it went into a 275 degree oven for an hour - allowing us to enjoy a relaxed cocktail hour. The dish was delicious - and good enough to serve to company. 
We also made one change in ingredients. It's not easy for us to find habaneros or scotch bonnet peppers so we used 3 peppers from the Thai pepper I've kept going for 3 years.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Made last night and followed the recipe close-ish. Fantastic results! Subbed a big squeeze (2 tsp maybe) of anchovy paste for the whole anchovies. Used 8 oz mushrooms and a 12 oz jar of roasted red peppers. Subbed a large hot jalapeño and three ripe serranos for the habanero. The pan was juicy enough without adding broth or water. Served with warm tortillas and walkerswood scotch bonnet hot sauce. Different, and very tasty!

commentBody: First time ever commenting. Scandalized that this recipe isn’t viral. I had no peppers whatsoever in a jar or fresh. Swapped for Juanita Piquante peppers and some ancient sun dried tomatoes in oil from the fridge. My thighs had bones. Cooked longer, added water as suggested. Covered a bit at the end. The flavors, the transformation from one thing to another, the forgiving nature of the ingredients because the end transcends the parts. This recipe should be cooked.

Instead of the serrano pepper, I used a jalapeno pepper.

commentBody: SO good! I used skin-on bone-in thighs and fish sauce (1 Tbsp) instead of anchovies. My chili was hotter than expected so I added a splash of cream as well. I highly suggest squeezing a good amount of lime juice into the sauce before serving. Nothing was left!

Not bad, but not memorable enough to make again given the many better weeknight chicken dishes out

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
@Jess Perfect recipe that I recommend generalizing as an approach to sauce and braising with high heat broil finishing (for crunch and color) as a platform for different flavor and texture combinations. Not surprising given its origins.
- In one week, I used zucchini, eggplant, mushrooms, and cabbage in various combinations. Cabbage + mushroom was the weakest but still edible combination. 
- Fresh oregano most nights but dill for a twist - all worked well. Further accentuation with preserved lemon finish also worked well.
- Simmering the sauce with schmaltz from chicken or pan juices from roasted fish added richness I would lost from an adjacent roasting approach over the single pan one. Why separate? Timing and volume. 
- Lastly, real anchovies and not anchovie paste (too little) or whole dried anchovies (too much) commonly found in ethnic Asian and African stores. Trust me.

commentBody: My husband and I were not fans of this dish at all, which surprised me based on the reviews. The texture of the mushrooms combined with the roasted red peppers did not sit well with me and it felt off balance. We didn’t see the comment to add a splash of vinegar, so maybe that would improve the flavor? I could see this working with tomatoes instead of peppers, but of course that would completely change the dish. It just wasn’t for me.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody:
This was great! Our family loved it! 

I think I overcooked the mushrooms, though. They were reduced too much. I tried re-hydrating them with chicken broth, but they came out a little too salty (and our family loves salt!). So I'd definitely stick with using 1/4 cup water, and not chicken broth, at the end if you need more liquid. I'd also recommend using low-sodium chicken broth for this recipe. Plenty of flavor and salt still I'm sure.

recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe faithfully except for substituting a large jalapeño, seeds and veins, for the habanero—way too hot for me. It was delicious and agree that there is a lot of flavor but it just seems like the recipe had to work really hard to achieve that flavor—packing it in with salty miso plus anchovies! Without the splash of sherry vinegar, which was not in the ingredients list, this would have been just meh. To be frank, I could have achieved the same flavor bomb with a large spoonful of Better Than Bouillon! And now I’m going to be bumped from this subscription for this terrible thought!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: This was great! We used habanero (seeded), a full tin of anchovies (1.73 oz., drained of oil), 12 oz jar of peppers, only 1/2 tsp cumin, 16 oz. Brown mushrooms, 2 very large cloves of garlic - sliced, and white miso. Didn’t have basil, so skipped that, but wish I’d thought of adding the parsley we did have on hand! Over-cooked it a bit due to a delay in putting the rice on, but the dish still held together very well with some added water. Served with lime wedges and habanero salsa.  Next time, leave in 1/2 the habanero seeds and put the rice on earlier :)

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: I agree with others: this WAS a umami flavor bomb! However, I also found it a bit too salty with 2tbsp red miso and anchovy paste (which I subbed instead of only anchovy filets). Suggestions for next time: only 1 tbsp miso. Sautée mushrooms after chicken to increase umami from Maillard reaction. Add beer instead of water to balance sweetness of roasted peppers and even more umami. Sub Cayenne pepper instead of habanero or Scotch bonnet if (like me) you can't take quite that much heat.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Spicy Chicken Thighs and Mushrooms Recipe
commentBody: Made this recipe yesterday. it looked beautiful but probably because of adjustments was so disappointing. My suggestion is don't do what I did: don't skimp on mushrooms (I only had 8 ounces), don't add all of two 12 ounce jars of roasted tomatoes (especially if short on mushrooms), carefully count the anchovies since in my case they were stuck together and I must have used to many! (sauce was too salty even though I added none, plus anchovies taste was overwhelming), poor your splash of apple cider vinegar into a separate vessel before adding (I am sure my splash was too big), don't be afraid to add the full teaspoon of cumin, don't substitute jalepeno pepper without seeds (no hotness at all in this version). The flavors were too strong. I plan to cook additional mushrooms

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Mbatata (Sweet Potato Cookies) Recipe
commentBody:
I am curious at the five star rating this recipe enjoys, not because the flavors aren’t promising, but because the dough required so much flour to effectively roll out that the texture and taste were impacted. Once rolled, the transfer was messy. I ended up doing a drop cookie which I then flattened with a tea glass.  

If I attempt this recipe again—and I doubt I will be inclined to do, given the time it took to fuss with the dough—I will roll out the dough on the same parchment I’ll bake with, cut shapes in place, and transfer the entire sheet to the pan. That’s the only way I can envision these working.

The lemon glaze was scrumptious, however. I had to resist the urge to consume it by the spoonful.

author: Yewande Komolafe

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Kelewele-Spiced Pork Chops and Pears Recipe
commentBody:
Made this last night (November 16, 2025).  

I found some of the instructions confusing, specifically what was considered the marinade.  So in that respect, I sort of “winged it” (something I frequently do when cooking).  I wound up adding all the ingredients to my large bowl when marinating the meat.  

Found holding the chops on edge to sear the fat caps problematic.  I tried, but don’t think I really got them seared.  And in the end I do not think it made a difference.  I did follow the directions with regards to searing one side five to six minutes and just “kissing” the flip side for a minute. I left the plate with the chops on my stovetop’s warmer section while working with the scallions and pears.

I wish I could include photos here, but I did post them to Instagram and Facebook.  Even though i didn’t follow the instructions to to the letter, the results tasted fantastic. 

A caution to anyone trying this recipe - the chop must be thick!  One of my worries was would the chop taste dried out? It did not!  It was fully cooked but moist.  I’m certain that a thinner chop would have been overdone.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Harissa Shrimp With Greens and Feta Recipe
commentBody:
Found this while looking for a Mediterranean-ish sheet pan shrimp recipe and was pleased to discover we had everything (except the limes—subbed lemons) and it was DELICIOUS! Served over pearl couscous with a tomato/cucumber/chickpea salad. 

Keep an eye on your kale during the broil: our broiler has both high and low settings and without guidance beyond “broil,” I set it to high and slightly burned the kale by the time the shrimp were done. author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Harissa Shrimp With Greens and Feta Recipe
commentBody: Thank you so much to everyone who offered tips on here! So helpful. This recipe called to me but I was unsure how the final product would turn out. It was delicious and unique! Absolutely separate the greens and the shrimp. Marinated overnight as well personally. My tips :: 1. Roast kale with onion (raw onion no thank you) at 400 for about 12 minutes. Toss in the middle and then transfer into the serving bowl once done. 2. On the same pan, do the shrimp under the broiler three minutes per side. 3. Serve over quinoa! Quinoa with seafood is so yum. Didn't have time to grab cilantro so I used mint in a pinch. And absolutely feta and salted avocado. My only thing was the dish cooled down quite quickly so it wasn't really hot ... But that's not too big of a deal. My cousin was saying it would be yummy cold and then I started fantasizing about it as a beach lunch for the summer! A keeper, I'd say. Thank you Yewande for another delish recipe! One of the best chefs of NYT Cooking undoubtedly. xo

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Harissa Shrimp With Greens and Feta Recipe
commentBody: I put the kale and shrimp on two separate pans. I roasted the kale on the top rack 10 min, according to the directions. Then I put the kale on the bottom rack, and shrimp on the top rack to broil. I broiled the shrimp on low for 3 min, then turned it up to high for a min and a half. I took the shrimp out, and broiled the kale on low for about 30 seconds, which browned it a little. If I followed this as written, I think the whole dish would have turned to ash.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Harissa Shrimp With Greens and Feta Recipe
commentBody:
-Wanted more kale. Would use 12 oz next time. Toss halfway through, it shrinks down quickly. 

-Not a huge fan of raw red onion, though I see it’s place here. Tossed it on the pan along w the shrimp for a 4 min broil. Mellowed the bite, but still had a bit of crunch. Would double. 

-The feta threw me. It seemed out of place. I was wrong!  If you can find sheep or goat milk feta in a block in the brine, it is a creamy, tangy, salty, flavor bomb and it makes the dish.

-Cilantro yes. Avocado optional, IMO. 
-Served over quinoa.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Harissa Shrimp With Greens and Feta Recipe
commentBody: I live in an apartment with a 3/4 sized oven and no broiler; so, I've become proficient at workarounds. I kept all the ingredients but cooked them differently. First, I marinated the shrimp for a couple hours. I sautéed chard stems and red onion with black pepper for 7-8 minutes and then added the chard leaves. I added about 1/2 cup of hot water and a lid to hurry the leaf wilting process. Added the shrimp and marinade; when it was time to flip the shrimp, I added cilantro and feta. Served avocado at the table. Delicious.

commentBody:
What a great meal but get ready for spicey! I used Gouchujang instead of harissa. Worked great. Not sure if cutting it back a bit would reduce spiciness or not. Served over basmati rice.
Agree with other comments to really watch the broil. Use low vs high to avoid the risk of burning the kale. 
Also suggest cutting the red onions back unless you are a real fan of them. 
I think next time I make this I will add chickpeas during the broiling phase.  Might be able to sub that for the rice.

commentBody:
The flavors in this dish are incredible-  I'm not sure I've ever stuffed shrimp into my mouth so quickly! The toppings give this a distinctly latin vibe, with the harissa providing a nice base layer of warmth. Also drizzled some Kefir. Yum! 

I was wary of burning the chard, so I placed the shrimp in the middle of the pan and the greens off to the side. I ended up slightly overcooking the shrimp and the greens never browned, but mixed all together with the toppings it was still delicious.

recipeTitle: Harissa Shrimp With Greens and Feta Recipe
commentBody: A great dinner which can be fast unless you make your own harissa like I did. It’s worth it but it would be best to make it the morning of.  We used Kale and overcooked it a bit (we like it well done) and had some fresh Maui fillets so cut them into chunks instead of defrosting shrimp. With the 40 min marinade in the harissa mix it was really good! Used avocado & chopped onion for toppings- husband added some hot sauce as I made the harissa @ medium heat. Yum.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Harissa Shrimp With Greens and Feta Recipe
commentBody: A great recipe if you know how fast your oven’s broiler cooks. I didn’t realize how quickly the kale would cook- should have taken it out after the first 10 minutes in the oven. It was burning in about one minute under the broiler; my extra large “jumbo” shrimp were done in 5 minutes. I didn’t have feta and used castelvetrano olives for a pop of salt - worked great. I recommend it if you don’t eat dairy or don’t have the feta and don’t want to make another trip to the store. I’ll make this again when I have feta now that I have the timing dialed in.

author: Yewande Komolafe

commentBody:
This was very tasty but had far too few greens. I am cooking in what I consider camp situation, poorly stocked grocery so had to make do with Earthbound Power Greens, a mix of baby kale, spinach, and chard—that worked, but I doubled the quantity called for, and we definitely wanted more. I forgot the water but that did not matter. I served it over quinoa, and used honey instead of sugar, as others had suggested. Very good. I will definitely be making this again, especially now that my CSA is delivering lots of greens.

BTW, I used the recalibration tool as I am cooking for just two. I live that feature. Thanks NYTC!

commentBody: My harissa does not "deepen in flavor once it’s kissed by the heat of a broiler," as the notes suggest. It is HOT. commentBody: Made it as written and it just tasted of molasses - which I do enjoy, but don't want to drink. I had to add more milk, tea and spices to make it drinkable. I think the brown sugar would get you closer to the flavor of the caramelized milk.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Shai Magnan (Sudanese Burned Milk Tea) Recipe
commentBody: This is a rich, delicious, well rounded cup of tea. Brewing the tea in the milk and simmering the milk for so long adds body, and reduces the tannic flavor. I did half maple syrup and half light brown sugar because that's what I had, and it turned out great. To deepen the flavor, I first reduced the maple syrup, and added brown sugar and let that slightly caramelize. Then I added in a little bit of milk first to dissolve the sugars, then added in the rest of the milk and followed as is.

commentBody: I scaled this back by 50% for 2 servings and made it exactly as the recipe was written choosing molasses for the sweetener option in the "either / or", whole milk (cow dairy) and Smith Teamaker's Kandy black tea for tea leaves. commentBody: I spent time in Kenya & East Africa in the early 1980s and fell in love with Chai ya tengawizi, which is made very similar to this Sudanese tea. Nzuri sana.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Roasted Vegetables and Polenta Recipe
commentBody: As someone who loves to roast vegetables, I struggled with this recipe's technique. The vegetables steamed under the foil, and when they went under the broiler, the outer edges of the brussels blackened before the shallots or sweet potatoes got any browning at all. I kept my sweet potato chunks small, but with everything being tossed in the polenta, it didn't seem there was enough surface area to achieve browning. Next time I'd roast the veggies separately.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Roasted Vegetables and Polenta Recipe
commentBody: The best recipe of this kind is Martha Rose Shulman's Roasted Root Vegetables with Polenta. The polenta is cooked separately in a 2 quart baking dish and the vegetables are roasted on a sheet pan at the same time time.  The proportions for the polenta are 1 cup polenta to 1 quart (4 cups) water. The result is foolproof decliciousness. If you line the sheet pan with parchment paper, the cleanup is super easy.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: One-Pan Roasted Vegetables and Polenta Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as directed with the exception of using dried rubbed sage instead of fresh. 35 minutes in the oven and 6 under the broiler on "low". Absolutely delicious and everything cooked perfectly. If I'm picking nits, it might be just a bit too lemony with the zest and juice of a whole lemon, next time I might try half a lemon and see how it turns out. Four servings might be a bit optimistic, it's more like two servings and a bit left over. If you wanted to keep it vegan you could leave out the goat cheese but it adds a wonderful richness. This one is going in the regular rotation!

commentBody: Delicious. I am not a fan of sweet potatoes, so I substituted diced delicate squash. Otherwise, I made this exactly the way Yewande suggested. It’s delicious. If you’re expecting creamy polenta (and doubling the liquid  as some suggest) you’re fundamentally changing the dish. The polenta is delicious with the 2-to-1 ratio. It leans chewier and absorbs the flavors in the vegetable concoction. Thank you for the lemon, Yewande. I might reduce the sage next time because that is such a distinctive flavor. But this is a delicious recipe as it is written. Try it that way.

The biggest problem for me though was how dry everything was. I'm not sure how I would fix this.

Skip the aluminum foil or parchment.

The result was not bland, but had no real defining favor element for all the effort required to make it. It was a fair amount of prep and there are many more tasty salmon recipes that do not require as much effort nor as many ingredients. The fish itself was tasty, but the sauce we rated “meh.”

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Salmon and Green Beans in Red Pepper Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This is so-so. I do not understand the rave reviews. While I am not anti-anchovy, I agree with Anti-Anchovy's comments. I like a lot of flavor, including spice heat, in my mouth, but this was unbalanced with too much heat and too much saltiness. Whatever you do, please do not waste beautiful salmon on this recipe as I did. (Copper River King.) Use a lesser salmon because the sauce will completely overpower the salmon. Unlike other commentators, I will not make this again.

commentBody: Made last night exactly as written but only used 1/2 of a seeded habanero.   Result was terrific.   My wife loved it and described it as “very clean”. author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Salmon and Green Beans in Red Pepper Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
Based on the reviews this recipe was loved or despised. We really enjoyed this dish - give it a try. 
Some suggestions based on the negative comments: I drained the oil used to brown the salmon to reduce the fishy taste some reviewers noticed. Strongly recommend skinless salmon (the freshest you can find). I cooked the pepper mixture with the anchovies and miso first to mellow the raw onion flavor and added a tablespoon of tomato paste and a tsp of curry powder, onion powder and garlic powder. Let that cook for 5-10 mins and the flavors meld beautifully. Taste as you go and add whatever flavors you like! I added twice the anount of green beans and 1 cup water after the pepper mixture lost its ‘sharpness’ and simmered until the green beans softened. Then added the fish and covered for a few minutes. Finished with some fresh lemon. Delicious with rice or crusty bread.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Salmon and Green Beans in Red Pepper Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Just made this last night. We are fortunate to have very easy access to excellent salmon, always looking for alternative presentations that really work well, and this one really does.  I would suggest removing the skin, cubing the salmon, and adding to the thickening simmering sauce 5-6 minutes prior to serving. Use more salmon than suggested to feed a crowd and add more beans as well, there is plenty of sauce to share.   The cubed salmon cooks rapidly and is much easier to eat. The skin, even if crispy from its initial sear, rarely retains any crisp and soggy salmon skin is not my cup of tea.  The flavors meld well, even better the next day.

commentBody: To be clear, by "green mangoes" the recipe no doubt means unripe mangoes, a common ingredient in many Swahili dishes.  Urojo is tart and tangy.  In Zanzibar, much of the pleasure of enjoying mix comes from selecting the savory "bitings" to top it with, such as kachori, cassava chips, and mishkaki (bits of skewered goat or beef often being grilled at a stand next to the mix vendor).

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Zanzibari Mix (Urojo) Recipe
commentBody:
@ Samantha: Green mangoes are a different variety of mango, common in East Africa. They are green when ripe, and are tart and sweet. (You determine ripeness by smell and touch.) They're one of the first things I get when I return as I rarely find them Stateside. Things may be changing. I use to only find the sweet variety of passion fruit in the US. Now, I can get both tart and sweet varieties. 
If you can't find green mangoes, add fresh lime juice to mango to approximate the flavor profile.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Smashed Chicken Meatballs with Suya and Charred Corn Recipe
commentBody: I found the recipe a bit too back and forth so I’ve simplified it.  Mix the peanut butter concoction in a small saucepan so the heat will help it mix easily. Added a dash of tamari and gojugong to it. Put 2-3 spoons of liquid peanut butter mixture in large bowl with ground chicken, spinach (didn’t have kale), extra ginger, a few dashes of garlic, paprika and onion salt along with a dash of cayenne and mixed with hands. Cooked the meatballs like it said. Added a little honey to warm peanut butter mixture. Drained the meatballs on paper towels. Added frozen corn to the pan and browned some, then added a little chicken stock and lime juice to scrape the pan with the corn and bits. Plate with lettuce, meatballs, corn and drizzle peanut butter mixture over top. Served with a little rice. Leftovers are great for lettuce wraps for lunch!

commentBody: Fantastic -- not easy, there are a lot of steps and it takes time.  Did not try to cook meatballs and corn at the same time.  Took the meat balls out after browning, cooked the corn at high temperture to char, then turned down and pushed the corn to one side, added the meat balls and reheated before serving. author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Smashed Chicken Meatballs with Suya and Charred Corn Recipe
commentBody: Made these tonight and really loved the flavor profile! Used ground turkey instead of chicken, and farro in place of rice.  Also, didn’t have peanut butter so used PB Fit powder instead (just add water to reconstitute). I too used the air fryer instead of pan on the stovetop, and to respond to MSG, time in air fryer was 7-8 minutes and no smashing needed.  Just made irregularly shaped meatballs and slightly flattened them before cooking. Will definitely make again!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Smashed Chicken Meatballs with Suya and Charred Corn Recipe
commentBody: I made these as a weeknight meal after another long day.  To say that it made the day worth it would be an understatement.  The combo of peanut powder and fresh greens, along with the nuttiness of the dressing was ethereal.  A perfect reason to keep miso around.  I tweaked the recipe to make the meal more like a salad by using fresh spinach and kale, rather than cooked and slightly wilted from frozen.  Other than that adaptation, I made the recipe as is and will definitely make this again!

commentBody: IMHO - there are far too many ingredients, and it takes much longer to prepare than stated. I don't think the (costly)miso added anything as it was overpowered by other ingredients. author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Smashed Chicken Meatballs with Suya and Charred Corn Recipe
commentBody: This is a unique and delicious recipe! I substituted Impossible beef for the chicken to make it vegetarian and roasted delicata squash for the corn since my garden is overflowing with it. I skipped the rice to avoid the carbs, but my husband said he wanted it with pita bread next time (and there will be a next time!). Be careful when grinding the suya spice - I went a little too far and almost turned it into peanut butter. Very adaptable recipe and quick enough for a weeknight dinner.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Suya Spice Recipe
commentBody:
This is terrific stuff. I thought, 1/4 cup of peanuts sounds way too small a batch; plus the recipe only said “makes 4 servings.” Yah but, how *much* is that? I doubled the recipe and, oh boy, 1/4 cup peanuts is plenty. I wound up with about 1.25 cups of the final mix in a doubled recipe. || 
I also replaced half the paprika with chipotle powder, to keep the smoke but amplify the heat; and kicked in some piquin chile molido at the end. (I likes the spicy.) Love this mix and can’t wait to try it on everything, veggies too. Popcorn!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Zanzibari Pizza Recipe
commentBody: I have made this twice. The first time, I used a large fry pan and it didn’t seem that the egg cooked all the way through. Then I used a panini press/foreman grill and it was way better. We also thought it needed a sauce and opted to make a tamarind chutney that was the perfect complement.

recipeTitle: Sticky Rice Cakes With Sausage and Greens Recipe
commentBody: Replaced the sausage with tofu. I cut it into batons, squeezed it dry, then crumbled it into a cast iron skillet with 3T sunflower oil. Followed the instructions otherwise. The sauce is pretty mild. I forgot I have already made this once! I’ll zhuzh it up next time. I’d add vinegar/sugar/salt to the sauce to keep it flavorful (but not spicy). Maybe increase the sauce 1.5x. Added broccolini in place of the bok choy. Both are good, but the recipe is highly adaptable. Almost added some kale, but kept it to the single veg. Don’t sleep on the chili crisp, that’s what shifts it from kid-friendly to adult-tantalizing. Clear out your fridge, and believe in yourself—you got this!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Sticky Rice Cakes With Sausage and Greens Recipe
commentBody: As a Korean who grew up on tteokbokki this is an interesting version. Koreans also make “ketchup tteokbokki” that is child-friendly but I noticed even these ones usually do include a tbsp or so of gochujang in addition to the ketchup. My child doesn’t like spicy so I decided to try this recipe. When I tasted it I thought it lacked a bit of something so ended up adding some agave syrup, msg, and soy sauce. Also added a handful more tteok as it seemed too little for the meat and sauce. This was fine for both my child and myself (better after adding chili crisp) but if it were for me I’d stick to the gochujang version

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Sticky Rice Cakes With Sausage and Greens Recipe
commentBody: Eerily, I made something very similar to this last week and then had my phone present this video to me today. Not sure if it’s a case of having seen the video before and only registering it in my subconscious, or my phone spying on my real life and feeding me what it considers relevant content… In any case, the sauce starts similarly with ground pork, diced onion, and chopped garlic, but then I added a hefty helping of gochujang, mixed with soy sauce, fish sauce, sesame oil, sugar, and water. Used rice coins instead of cylinders because that’s what I had, and threw in a whole bunch of chopped bok choy leaves towards the end. I had jokingly called the dish Korean bolognese but now I see that name’s already taken on this app, too! So I guess I’m disappointingly unoriginal. Figured I’d share anyway because it was super tasty.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Iced Coconut Limeade Recipe
commentBody: So tart and refreshing, and easy! My edits: made with 6 XXL limes (yes, peels and all), full-fat coconut milk, 50/50 herb mix--less than 1 cup each to avoid overpowering the lime, and only about 3/4 cup sugar because I don't like sweet drinks. I agree with others it seems a waste to throw out the solids! It is excellent as is, but I like cocktails and  taste-tested with white tequila and rum, and rum was definitely the winner. Mine was not as bright green as the photo but was still pretty.

recipeTitle: Iced Coconut Limeade Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice this week. It does get noticeably bitter on day 2, but the addition of some simple syrup tamed it. For the second batch I used leftover sweetened coconut cream from a cake project which I mixed with water until I got the volume of 1 can of coconut milk. Since the coconut cream is very sweet, I adjusted the volume of sugar based on the amount of cream I used, but to equal the amount stated in the ingredients. It was just as delicious.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Lemon-Pepper Zucchini Pasta With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
LOVED it!  The miso+lemon is an awesome flavor, and goes incredibly well with pasta.  And the salting does help the zucchini stand up to the cooking.  This dish punches above its prep time.  

I made it largely as written but with mods per the comments:
-subbed tarragon, basil, and some mint from the garden (my dill’s done) 
-rinsed the zucchini THEN toweled off; salt level in finished dish was perfect. 
-only used enough water to make the sauce.  Miso needs some water to dissolve; the rest is up to how you like it.  
-And added leftover chicken breast just before the final combination, in zucchini-size pieces.  Worked great.  

This got very good household reviews & I agreed:-)

commentBody: It's amazing that this gets 5 stars as there are many issues with the recipe including some confusion noted in other comments. commentBody: Made this tonight. Did not salt the zucchini or the pasta water. Browned zucchini in a little oil, removed from pan, then sautéed some chopped onion, broccolini and mushrooms and put that in bowl w zucchini and dill. author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Lemon-Pepper Zucchini Pasta With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Top marks! I added more zucchini because it cooks down a lot, and it was delicious. I also threw in some snow peas for more green and a little crunch, some jarred roasted artichokes for a little tang, and added fresh oregano to the spice bowl. My miso was less salty than most brands, but taste it first along with the pecorino/parmesan to watch the sodium levels. Definitely top it with a healthy spray of fresh ground black pepper! This will be in constant summer rotation.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Lemon-Pepper Zucchini Pasta With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent.  Took pasta quantity down for 2, using 1-1/2 zucchinis, 1 T butter and 2 T miso but used 3 small garlic cloves!  Reduced pasta water and cheese as well. After browning the zucchini, I sautéed mushrooms, keeping the zucchini warm in the bowl with the zest, dill and pepper. At the end, I squeezed half a lemon before serving and adding the dill and more cheese. Next time, I would up the lemon zest and consider adding lemon paste at the same time of the miso, as we love lemon!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Pork and Plums With Fresh Herbs Recipe
commentBody: Just cooked this with a few modifications, and it turned out delicious.  Harris Teeter didn’t have pork belly, so I got a strip of fatty bacon and lean pork chops instead.  I also used only two large, fist-size black plums, but added two small zucchinis, a vidalia onion, and two small sweet peppers.  As for the sequence, first I made the sauce as suggested in the recipe, then cooked one bacon strip cut in pieces and chopped onion for maybe 5 minutes, then added pork chops sliced in thumb-sized chunks and sliced sweet peppers.  Added a bit of sauce as there wasn’t enough fat in the wok.  Cooked for six minutes, then added two black plums cut in small pieces, cooked for a couple of minutes, then added sliced zucchini and the remaining sauce and cooked 5 minutes longer.  Added cilantro on top before serving.  Next time I might add a bit of honey or brown sugar, but it was really good as described.

commentBody: Made this for dinner last week and we loved it. I used a wok, and also opted for pork tenderloin (pork belly was unavailable and I’d taken note of the comments about too much fat). Other than that, made the recipe exactly as written. It was delicious. Salty, sour, sweet, spicy. In other words, umami goodness. I think this would work great, too, with cherries. Maybe sub in duck breast for the pork? Also a combination of chicken and peaches would be nice. In any event, the flavors are great, and we’ll definitely make this again.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Pork and Plums With Fresh Herbs Recipe
commentBody: It made a good meal but the recipe needs some tweaks.  Step 4 should take much less time or the sauce will completely dry up, I added vermouth to keep things loose. Next time I’ll have some plum wine on hand and cook longer in step 3 and for less time in step 4. I used half the vinegar and it was perfect for us. The plums I used were very firm, I’m going to try next time with riper plums and see if they leave a richer sauce. The arugula was a perfect accompaniment but we dressed the leaves simply

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Pork and Plums With Fresh Herbs Recipe
commentBody:
Recipe says to mix all of the sauce ingredients ahead of time ... I'd go light on the red wine vinegar, because unless your plums are super sweet ... it can get awfully tangy.  
Served with rice and Asian stir fried green beans.  Quite delicious, if a bit tangy.  We used tiny Japanese Shiro plums (yellow) and subbed about 6 for each normal sized plum in the recipe (friend has a tree just loaded with these gorgeous little plums).  
We used a pork tenderloin and added a tiny bit of fat.  Delicious

Make sure you taste your plums first and adjust the sugar accordingly.

recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Pork and Plums With Fresh Herbs Recipe
commentBody:
This looks like a really good recipe.   It as I am on a low sodium diet for health reasons, I thought the nutritional information of a serving was a little low.

Fish sauce, one of my very favorite but hard to incorporate ingredients, has between 1400 and 1500 milligrams of sodium in a tablespoon.  And the recipe says it makes 4 servings.

But the nutritional information says the recipe makes 12 servings.

Please clarify which one is correct.  Thank you.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Pork and Plums With Fresh Herbs Recipe
commentBody: This really didn’t work. Meat was tough, fat did not stay tender, caramelization did not happen.  This seemed like an easier option to try than a long slow roast or braise, but the result after following recipe as written was inedible. At very least needs more detail about how much fat to trim, how much oil to pour off, etc. The pan was too crowded and keeping it low to keep meat  tender  meant the sauce never caramelized even after dumping half or more of the fat / liquid. & the meat still came out tough.

commentBody:
This was a hit!! Made the recipe exactly as written although I added 1/4 tsp Chinese five spice to the sauce. I also removed the rind from the pork belly along with a bit of fat and put that in a ceramic cast iron skillet in the oven at 450 for 30 minutes to create cracklin!
Served over basmati rice and a hispi cabbage chiffonade lightly sauteed in olive oil with the cracklin on the side.
One of the best NYT recipes!

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Stir-Fried Pork and Plums With Fresh Herbs Recipe
commentBody: When I saw that several commenters reported that the the pork belly was tough and rubbery, I was not surprised. So I covered the chunks of pork belly with cold water in a saucepan and brought it to a moderate boil for 10 minutes, then rinsed and covered them in cold water again and simmered them for an hour. I dried them completely before searing in a T. of oil in a stainless steel skillet until crispy. I thought the sauce was skimpy, so I added 2 T. of chicken stock. The result was perfection.

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Bratwurst With Caramelized Onions and Chakalaka Recipe
commentBody:
This is a great recipe. However, the order of cooking ingredients seems wrong to me. Per the recipe, I browned the buns and brats first, set them aside to sauté the onions. Everything was mise en place, so there was no wasted time. However, by the time the onions were sautéed and spiced, the brats and rolls were cold. 
I suggest sauteeing the onions first, stashing them in a warming drawer or 200deg. oven, then browning the rolls and brats. That way, everything is nice and warm when you eat it.
Btw, the spiced onions were delicious. 
Didn’t make the chakalaka as, here in the Northeast, in January, summer veggies are very pretty but tasteless. I’ll wait for my summer garden veggies to make that.

commentBody: For a little more authenticity, I’d swap the garam masala for curry powder and bay leaves - that will get you a better flavour profile. Yellow onions are more commonly used, but red onions would probably be delicious! Whatever you have can work honestly, as long as it’s tomatoey and spicy and simple and moreish! It’s not one exact dish, everyone has their own recipe. That said I’ve never heard of adding limes haha - I should try it. Can recommend hollowing out a bread roll and filling it with chakalaka as a snack/lunch while camping.

commentBody: I loved this!  We have so many seasonal veggies in our garden—tomatoes, peppers, carrots to use-- and I even added shredded cabbage. I also added a can of rinsed garbanzo beans. LOVE this on toast for breakfast in the morning. A great way to take a break from oatmeal this summer. My friend from S. Africa loves this Chakalaka.

commentBody: There are chilis in the recipe, via the suya spice blend. As described in the tip, cayenne and paprika are included and both are chilis. As far as missing chili powder, most chili powders are a spice blend which include a blend of chilis, but cumin, garlic, onions, etc. Since the the tip describes a blend itself, using chili powder would throw things off. Now, substituting another ground chili, say Allepo, that might be interesting...

author: Yewande Komolafe
recipeTitle: Pan-Seared Chicken With Mujdei Green Beans Recipe
commentBody:
I gave this recipe 5 stars, but I think its reviews are suffering from the American palate’s fear of garlic and the deceiving difficulty of making the mujdei. Here are some of my tips:

1. Don’t fear the garlic! Use the full 3/4 cups. Yes, it’s a lot. But without this quantity, the mujdei won’t emulsify and it won’t take on the thick, opaque, creamy texture that makes it shine. If you’re set on using less garlic, reduce the other mujdei ingredients proportionally.

2. Crush the garlic cloves and soak them in lemon juice for 10-20 minutes to mute the garlic’s bite. Cover the cloves in the juice of 2-3 lemons. The garlic will also neutralize the lemon juice’s bite and you can use it to top your plate in place of lemon wedges.

3. Achieving the creamy oil/garlic emulsion of the mujdei is difficult. You can cheat by using a high powered blender like a Vitamix, which also lets you skip grating/pressing the garlic. Follow the recipe’s instructions and ingredient orders for the mujdei, but blend at high speed instead of whisking.

4. The green bean salad may seem overdressed, but if you want a big punch of flavor, it’s actually great like this.

5. For the chicken, I prefer covering the thighs in my favorite dry rub and making them in the air fryer, which lets you make the beans and chicken at the same time. If you go this route, allow the beans to cool for about 10 min before dressing them. 

I hope these tips help and I’d love to know how they work for you!
